title: Super sector tipped as winner in CGT reform
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Tax & super Super sector tipped as winner in CGT reform afr.com John Kehoe and Lucas Baird 30 April 2026 19:00 GMT 887 words English AFNROL Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. The Albanese government has signalled to the $4.5 trillion superannuation sector that super funds are likely to be exempted from capital gains tax changes in the federal budget, allowing them to keep their existing 33 per cent discount on earnings. Labor is expected to announce in the May 12 budget that it is abandoning the 50 per cent discount for personal investors earning capital gains from property, shares and other assets held longer than 12 months, and reverting to the Keating-era pre-1999 inflation indexation model. Superannuation investments also receive a discount on capital gains. But industry sources, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said the government had not indicated that super funds would be affected by its overhaul of the CGT discount. The Labor government, which is close to union-aligned industry super funds, has told the sector that it should expect a quiet budget without major changes affecting them, two sources said. Financial Services Council policy director Chaneg Torres said the government’s rationale for changing the CGT was to address the intergenerational inequity that was especially acute in housing. “The case for extending CGT changes to superannuation or other asset classes outside of housing has not been made,” said Torres, whose organisation represents major retail funds including AMP, Colonial First State and Mercer. “The existing CGT discount plays an important role in supporting long-term capital investment across superannuation, managed funds, Australian infrastructure and other productive assets.” Industry and retail funds, as well as self-managed superannuation funds, are eligible for a 33 per cent discount on capital gains. Hence, the existing tax rate all super funds pay on capital gains is a flat 10 per cent, below the 15 per cent they currently pay on other income such as interest, rent and dividends, less any franking credits. One industry source said the existing 33 per cent discount for super funds was less generous than the 50 per cent discount for personal investments and worked out to about the same amount of tax paid as inflation indexation would. The Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia, Super Members Council and SMSF Association all declined to comment. A spokesman for Treasurer Jim Chalmers declined to comment. The government is seeking ways to boost revenue and address intergenerational inequity in the coming budget as it deals with a decade of deficits, rising inflation, stagnant productivity and public debt nudging $1 trillion. The 33 per cent discount for capital gains inside super funds was introduced in September 1999 by then-Liberal treasurer Peter Costello at the same time he initiated a 50 per cent discount for individuals. The government is considering reverting to former Labor prime minister and treasurer Paul Keating’s inflation indexation model, which protects investors against inflation and taxes only real gains. Chalmers flags ‘transition’ for existing investments On Thursday, Chalmers stopped short of guaranteeing that existing assets would be fully exempt from changes to the CGT discount, further fuelling speculation that future gains on existing investments will come under the new tax regime. At the same time, he signalled that the impact on existing assets would be minimal, playing down expectations that changes to both the CGT and negative gearing would raise much revenue for the government in the near term. When asked whether there would be a grandfathering of existing investments, Chalmers spoke of “transitional matters”. “Whenever anyone is thinking about these sorts of issues, some of these big tax reforms that have been speculated about, obviously people work through or think through some of those transitional matters,” he told a Commonwealth Bank of Australia podcast. The superannuation industry is already bedding down the tax increase for balances of more than $3 million and the introduction of payday super. It is preparing for Chalmers to tweak the performance test next week to make it easier for super funds to invest in emerging assets such as renewable energy, housing, venture capital and start-ups. One senior fund executive, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive issues, suggested that altering the CGT discount would require complex system and accounting changes similar to the clawback of concessions on super balances of more than $3 million. While that law primarily targeted wealthy SMSF holders, it is an administrative headache for traditional industry and retail funds that buy and sell assets based on what is best for the broader member base rather than any specific individual. Mark Molesworth, tax partner at BDO, said it would be easier to switch to inflation indexation for all assets and entities, including super funds. “Just thinking about legislative complexity, it’s going to be easier to switch indexation on and the discount off for [all entity types] because the provisions are the same, you don’t have separate discounting provisions for superannuation, you simply have a different rate.” Looking after industry fund ‘mates’ Shadow treasurer Tim Wilson said Labor would look after its mates at the industry super funds. “I am expecting industry super funds will have their CGT discounts and be able to write off losses akin to negative gearing,” he said. “Meanwhile, mums, dads, self-starters and SMSFs will be hit with higher taxes and pick up the bill.” Additional reporting: Michelle Bowes YXJ0aWNsZTpiZDgwNzg2Yi1iODBhLTQ2OGUtYjhjYi0xNWMyZmY4NWM3Yjg Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited Document AFNROL0020260503em4u000xe Actuaries Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more. summary: The Albanese government is expected to exempt superannuation funds from proposed capital gains tax (CGT) reforms, maintaining their current 33% discount on earnings. In contrast, the government plans to eliminate the 50% CGT discount for personal investors, reverting to an inflation indexation model. This move aims to address intergenerational equity while managing the government's revenue needs amidst rising public debt.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Superannuation funds likely to remain exempt from CGT changes in the upcoming budget.
Labor government plans to revert to an inflation indexation model for personal investors' CGT.
The 33% CGT discount for super funds supports long-term capital investment.
relevance: Understanding these tax reforms is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as they impact investment strategies and client financial planning.
